title: Gifts of Youth
explanation: This theme focuses on young people's energy, creativity, talents, ambitions, and challenges. In the exam, texts may discuss youth clubs, volunteering, unemployment, dreams, or how teenagers can serve society.

vocabulary.definition: the ability to develop and succeed
a strong desire to achieve something
a difficult task or problem
belief in your own abilities
the ability to produce new ideas
influence from friends of the same age

expressions: Young people can make a difference.
Youth is a period of discovery.
Students need guidance and opportunities.
examples: Many Moroccan students join clubs to develop their talents.
Volunteering helps young people build self-confidence.
Peer pressure can affect teenagers' choices.
writingTopics: How can young people contribute to their community?
Write about the challenges facing Moroccan youth.
Describe a young person who inspires you.
readingTips: Expect words about dreams, problems, clubs, and community service.
For attitude questions, check if the writer is hopeful or critical about youth.

title: Humour
explanation: Humour is about jokes, laughter, comedy, and the social value of smiling. Exam texts may show how humour reduces stress, improves relationships, or criticizes problems politely.

vocabulary.definition: the act of laughing
a person who makes people laugh
the ability to understand or make jokes
humour used to criticize society
funny and enjoyable
something that reduces worry

expressions: Laughter is good for health.
Humour can send a serious message.
A good joke should not hurt people.
examples: Humour helps students relax before exams.
Some comedians use satire to discuss social issues.
A person with a good sense of humour can make difficult moments easier.
writingTopics: Write a funny story that happened at school.
Is humour important in our daily life?
Can jokes sometimes hurt people?
readingTips: Pay attention to whether humour is presented as positive or harmful.
Vocabulary from context may include adjectives like amusing, ridiculous, or serious.

title: Education: Formal, Informal, and Non-formal
explanation: Education includes school learning, life experience, training centers, online courses, family learning, and community programs. Exam texts often discuss equal access, literacy, dropout, e-learning, or lifelong learning.

vocabulary.definition: learning in schools and universities
learning from family, friends, media, and experience
organized learning outside the school system
the ability to read and write
money given to help a student study
a student who leaves school before finishing

expressions: Education opens doors.
Learning does not stop at school.
Every child deserves access to education.
examples: Online learning can help students in remote areas.
Non-formal education gives adults a second chance.
Scholarships encourage talented students to continue their studies.
writingTopics: The importance of education for development.
Advantages and disadvantages of online learning.
How can Morocco reduce school dropout?
readingTips: Look for causes and effects when the text discusses dropout.
Distinguish between formal, informal, and non-formal examples.

title: Cultural Issues and Values
explanation: This theme deals with traditions, customs, identity, stereotypes, cultural diversity, and respect for difference. Exam texts may compare cultures or discuss how people keep their identity in a global world.

vocabulary.definition: a traditional way of behaving
a belief or practice passed from generation to generation
who a person or group is
an unfair fixed idea about a group
variety of people, cultures, or ideas
respecting people who are different

expressions: Culture shapes our identity.
Diversity enriches society.
Respect is the basis of coexistence.
examples: Moroccan culture is known for hospitality and diversity.
Stereotypes can create misunderstanding between people.
Young people can preserve traditions while learning from other cultures.
writingTopics: The importance of preserving cultural identity.
How can people fight stereotypes?
Write about a Moroccan tradition you value.
readingTips: For inference questions, check how the writer describes difference.
Reference words often refer to cultures, traditions, or groups.

title: Women and Power
explanation: This theme is about women's rights, equality, education, leadership, and empowerment. Exam texts may present successful women or discuss obstacles women face in education, work, or public life.

vocabulary.definition: giving people power, confidence, and opportunities
having the same rights and chances
the ability to guide or influence others
unfair treatment of a person or group
something important that someone succeeds in doing
a person others admire and follow

expressions: Educating girls benefits the whole society.
Women can be leaders in every field.
Equality means fair chances for all.
examples: Many Moroccan women have become successful entrepreneurs.
Education helps women participate in public life.
Discrimination prevents society from using all its talents.
writingTopics: Why is girls' education important?
Write about a woman who inspires you.
How can society support women's empowerment?
readingTips: Notice whether the text focuses on achievements, obstacles, or solutions.
Cause and effect questions may ask how education changes women's lives.

title: Science and Technology
explanation: This theme covers inventions, the internet, artificial intelligence, medicine, communication, and the effects of technology on daily life. Exam texts often ask students to balance advantages and disadvantages.

vocabulary.definition: a new idea, method, or product
a machine or tool
computer systems that do tasks needing human intelligence
using computer technology
control over personal information
a strong harmful need to do or use something

expressions: Technology is a tool, not a goal.
Innovation can improve people's lives.
Students should use the internet wisely.
examples: Smartphones help students access information quickly.
Too much screen time can harm concentration.
Artificial intelligence may change many jobs in the future.
writingTopics: Advantages and disadvantages of technology.
Should students use smartphones at school?
How can technology improve education?
readingTips: Look for contrast connectors when a text discusses pros and cons.
Author attitude may be balanced, optimistic, or worried.

title: Sustainable Development
explanation: Sustainable development means improving life today without destroying resources for future generations. Exam texts may discuss pollution, renewable energy, water, recycling, climate change, or local associations.

vocabulary.definition: using resources in a way that protects the future
damage caused to air, water, or land
energy from sources like sun, wind, or water
using waste materials again
long-term change in weather patterns
knowledge about an issue

expressions: Small actions can protect the planet.
Development should respect nature.
Environmental awareness begins at school.
examples: Students can organize campaigns to clean their neighborhood.
Solar energy is useful in sunny regions.
Saving water is important in many Moroccan cities.
writingTopics: How can students protect the environment?
Write about the importance of renewable energy.
What can people do to reduce pollution?
readingTips: Cause and effect questions are common in environmental texts.
Watch for numbers or examples that support the main idea.

title: Citizenship
explanation: Citizenship means belonging to a community and respecting rights and duties. Exam texts may discuss volunteering, voting, public property, tolerance, school clubs, and responsible behavior.

vocabulary.definition: a member of a country or community
something a person is allowed to have or do
something a person should do
helping others without payment
a duty to do something well
places or things owned by the community

expressions: Good citizens respect both rights and duties.
Volunteering teaches responsibility.
Public property belongs to everyone.
examples: Students show citizenship when they keep their school clean.
Volunteering develops solidarity among young people.
Respecting traffic rules is a civic duty.
writingTopics: What makes a good citizen?
The importance of volunteering.
How can students serve their community?
readingTips: Differentiate between rights and duties.
Matching exercises may use synonyms like responsibility and duty.

title: International Organizations
explanation: International organizations work across countries to solve global problems. Exam texts may mention education, health, peace, children, refugees, poverty, and humanitarian aid.

vocabulary.definition: a group working for a purpose
related to helping people in need
help, often food, money, or services
actions to maintain peace
a person forced to leave home because of danger
organized actions to reach a goal

expressions: Global problems need global cooperation.
Humanitarian work saves lives.
Organizations can support vulnerable people.
examples: Some organizations provide school supplies for poor children.
Humanitarian aid is important after natural disasters.
International cooperation can reduce poverty.
writingTopics: Why are international organizations important?
Write about an organization that helps people.
How can young people support humanitarian campaigns?
readingTips: Pay attention to the organization's purpose and target group.
In main idea questions, avoid focusing only on one example of aid.

title: Brain Drain
explanation: Brain drain is the migration of educated or skilled people to other countries for better opportunities. Exam texts may discuss causes, effects, and possible solutions.

vocabulary.definition: a person with special training or ability
a good chance to do something
to leave your country to live elsewhere
not enough of something
money or effort used to create future benefit
a person who comes back after living elsewhere

expressions: Brain drain can weaken local development.
Countries should create opportunities for talent.
Migrants can also send knowledge back home.
examples: Some doctors emigrate because they want better working conditions.
Creating research centers can reduce brain drain.
Returnees may bring new skills and ideas.
writingTopics: Causes and effects of brain drain.
How can countries encourage skilled people to stay?
Is migration always negative?
readingTips: Expect cause and effect vocabulary.
Separate causes, consequences, and solutions in your notes.

title: Migration
explanation: Migration means moving from one place to another, inside a country or abroad. Exam texts may discuss reasons for migration, integration, identity, remittances, and challenges.

vocabulary.definition: a person who moves to another place
a person who comes to live in a new country
becoming part of a community
money migrants send to their families
sadness because one misses home
to change to fit a new situation

expressions: Migration can bring both opportunities and difficulties.
Integration requires respect from both sides.
Many migrants work hard to support their families.
examples: Some young people move to cities to find work.
Migrants may face language problems in a new country.
Remittances can help families pay for education.
writingTopics: Why do people migrate?
Advantages and disadvantages of migration.
How can migrants integrate into a new society?
readingTips: Identify whether the text discusses internal or international migration.
Look for emotional words such as hope, fear, or homesickness.

title: Human Rights
explanation: Human rights are basic rights that all people should have, such as education, safety, dignity, equality, and freedom of expression. Exam texts may discuss children, women, people with disabilities, or social justice.

vocabulary.definition: the value and respect every person deserves
the right to act, speak, or think without unfair control
fair treatment
equal rights and chances
keeping someone safe
an action that breaks a right or rule

expressions: Human rights belong to everyone.
Rights come with responsibilities.
A fair society protects vulnerable people.
examples: Education is an important right for every child.
Discrimination is a violation of human dignity.
Awareness campaigns can help people understand their rights.
writingTopics: Why are human rights important?
How can schools teach respect for rights?
Write about equality in society.
readingTips: Texts may include examples of problems and solutions.
For author's attitude, look for words showing criticism of injustice.

title: Media and Communication
explanation: This theme covers television, newspapers, social media, advertising, communication skills, and the spread of information. Exam texts often discuss fake news, responsible use, and media influence.

vocabulary.definition: ways of sharing information such as TV, radio, newspapers, and internet
sharing ideas or information
false information presented as news
where information comes from
messages that promote products or ideas
the power to affect opinions or behavior

expressions: Responsible users check information before sharing it.
Media can educate and mislead at the same time.
Good communication prevents misunderstanding.
examples: Students should check sources before believing online news.
Social media helps people stay connected.
Advertising can influence what teenagers buy.
writingTopics: Advantages and disadvantages of social media.
How can students avoid fake news?
The role of media in education.
readingTips: Identify if the text is warning, informing, or persuading.
Vocabulary questions may use words such as source, reliable, or misleading.

title: Personal Values and Social Values
explanation: Values are principles that guide behavior. Personal values include honesty, patience, and hard work. Social values include solidarity, respect, tolerance, and cooperation.

vocabulary.definition: telling the truth and acting fairly
supporting people who need help
treating people politely and fairly
working together
being trusted to do what is right
the ability to wait or continue calmly

expressions: Values guide our choices.
Respect creates peaceful relationships.
A strong society is built on cooperation.
examples: Honesty is important in exams and daily life.
Solidarity appears when people help families in need.
Cooperation makes group projects successful.
writingTopics: Which value is most important for students?
How can schools teach social values?
Write about a situation where honesty was important.
readingTips: The text may give a story that teaches a value.
In inference questions, identify the lesson behind the action.
